source.title: Triangulating a cognitive control network using diffusion-weighted magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and functional MRI

confidence_rationale: Provides the strongest anatomical evidence for a dedicated BG circuit serving the action commitment gate function. The IFC-STN hyperdirect pathway is anatomically validated (DWI tractography, 7-9/10 subjects) and functionally distinct from the canonical striato-pallidal Go pathway. The key architectural point: the commit gate uses a different BG pathway (STN-based) than the thought-loop gate (SNr-based), supporting anatomical separability.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Right IFC connects directly to STN via a white matter tract (hyperdirect pathway) bypassing the striatum. IFC-STN activation correlates with stop-signal reaction time; preSMA specifically activates with conflict-induced slowing. STN excites GPi, suppressing thalamocortical motor output — implementing a global action halt.
mapping.ree_translation: IFC-STN hyperdirect pathway = anatomical substrate of the action commitment gate. STN→GPi suppression = mechanism by which the gate arrests irreversible motor commitment. preSMA conflict detection = pre-commit error channel signaling (conflict before commitment recruits the gate). Key REE implication: action commitment gate uses STN-based hyperdirect circuit, anatomically separable from SNr-based cognitive BG loops.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Paper focuses on the motor/action domain; does not address sensorium or thought-loop gates. The STN-based commit gate pathway is architecturally distinct from the SNr-based circuit in O'Reilly/Frank PBWM — this distinction matters for REE implementation. Transfer risk: human fMRI/DWI, but anatomy is direct.
mapping.source_context: Journal of Neuroscience, 2007. Empirical study combining DWI tractography and fMRI to identify and validate the IFC-STN-preSMA network.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (empirical, DWI anatomical validation). High mapping fidelity for action commitment gate specifically. Low transfer risk — direct human neuroscience with anatomical confirmation.
